summary: I am a sixth-generation Montgomery Countian, deeply rooted in this place where my family has lived for generations. My ancestor Enoch Smith had the first land grant here and built the first stone home in Montgomery County. Growing up here, I witnessed many changes, especially around Mount Sterling and the local traditions like Court Day. The county holds a lot of my family history and personal memories.

Enoch Smith was one of my ancestors who was at Boonesborough with Daniel Boone and received the very first land grant in Kentucky, right here in Montgomery County. He built the first stone home in the county and is buried in a private family cemetery nearby. My family has tried to preserve his gravesite, and many of his descendants still live in the area. His historical significance is something I feel strongly about preserving for future generations.

Boonesborough is historically important because my ancestor Enoch Smith was there with Daniel Boone during the early settlement days. It was part of the frontier where the first land grants in Kentucky were made. This place connects my family to the very beginnings of Kentucky’s history and settlement.

Daniel Boone was a key figure in Kentucky’s early history and was at Boonesborough with my ancestor Enoch Smith. Boone’s exploration and settlement efforts are part of the stories passed down in my family. His role in Kentucky’s frontier history is something I grew up hearing about through my family’s connection to those times.

My grandmother was a member of the Daughters of the American Revolution, and I joined the organization through my lineage to Enoch Smith’s father. This connection to the DAR sparked my interest in family history and genealogy. It was a way for me to honor my ancestors and their role in American history.

John Newton Lee was my father, named after his great-grandfather. He was deeply interested in our family history and would recite our lineage from memory, even though he never wrote it down. He was also our taxi driver during high school and spent time driving us around the area, including through the cemetery where some ancestors are buried.

Mount Sterling is the town near where I grew up and where many family activities took place. It was the trade location for Court Day, where traders from the mountains would come to exchange goods. I attended Mount Sterling city schools through eighth grade and later Montgomery County High School. The town has been central to my life and family history.

Sharpsburg is a nearby town connected to Mount Sterling by Highway 11, close to where my family farm was located. It’s part of the local geography that shaped my childhood and the routes we traveled, especially before Highway 11 was rerouted. The area between Mount Sterling and Sharpsburg includes places like the Somerset Christian Church, which was near our farm.

Somerset Christian Church is a landmark near our family farm, located between Mount Sterling and Sharpsburg. It was part of the local community landscape where I grew up. The church’s proximity to our farm made it a familiar place during my childhood in Montgomery County.

Court Day was originally a one-day event in October when mountain traders came to Mount Sterling to exchange goods like sorghum and honey. It wasn’t always a festival, and in my early memories from the 1950s and 60s, it was mostly a men’s event held on Locust Street, which was a rough area back then. I first participated around 1961 or 62 as a high school sophomore, volunteering at a school booth. Over time, Court Day grew into a larger festival with more crowd control and community involvement.

Locust Street was the original hub for Court Day trading and gatherings, but back in the 1950s it was in disrepair and known for bars and some unsavory places. My father would drive us there on Sunday evenings to see the traders who came from the mountains and parked overnight. It was not a place for children or women at the time, and my mother kept a tight rein on me. Locust Street was also home to the stockyards and was near Monarch Mill and Queen Street.

Highway 11 was the main road near our family farm before it was rerouted, and the old section is now called Old Highway 11. It connects Mount Sterling to Sharpsburg and beyond, running close to places like the Somerset Christian Church. Our farm was just a short distance from this highway, making it a familiar route during my upbringing.

Monarch Mill was a landmark near Locust Street and the stockyards in Mount Sterling. It was part of the block where trading and other activities happened during Court Day. I remember it being close to Queen Street and the Civic Center area, marking a familiar part of the town’s layout.

The Boy Scouts of America were involved in Court Day as one of the groups running fundraisers. I recall a serious incident where a woman associated with a scouting group was badly burned in a grease fire during the event. That incident highlighted the need for better crowd and emergency control at Court Day, leading to more regulations in later years.

I attended Montgomery County High School after completing eighth grade in Mount Sterling city schools. During my sophomore year around 1961 or 62, my class had a booth at Court Day, and I volunteered to help, which was a big deal for my mother. The high school was an important part of my teenage years and connected me to community events like Court Day.

I attended Mount Sterling city schools through the eighth grade before moving on to Montgomery County High School. The city schools were where I first experienced the local community and traditions, including running down to Court Day during lunch breaks. Kentucky history was part of the curriculum, and my father would often supplement what we learned with stories from our family’s past.
